dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at [CITY], Apt #10.
Hi, my name is James. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having difficulty breathing and I've been coughing really bad for the last two weeks.
Can you describe specifically where the shortness of breath and coughing are originating?
It's coming from both lungs, and I'm coughing up red sputum.
Have you tried your inhaler, and is this episode worse than previous ones you've had?
Yes, I have COPD and the inhaler isn't helping. This is the worst episode I've had.
Can you tell me your name, today's date, and where we are?
My name is Robert. Today is October 31st, 2020, and I'm in my home in [CITY].
How are you feeling in terms of alertness, and is anything making you uncomfortable or confused?
I'm alert, and while I have trouble breathing, I'm not confused or unwell otherwise.
Do you feel any difficulty swallowing or a blockage in your throat?
No, my throat doesn't feel blocked or painful.
Your airway is patent and maintainable with no signs of obstruction.
It looks like you're breathing very quickly. When did the shortness of breath start?
It started gradually over the last two weeks and has gotten progressively worse.
Have you been coughing up sputum?
Yes, I've been coughing up red sputum, particularly with each cough.
His radial pulse is strong, regular, and equal in both arms. I'll check other vital signs now.
Respirations are 50 per minute and shallow.
Pulse rate is 129 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is currently 124 over 77.
Glucose reading is 92 mg/dL and stable.
Are you experiencing any chest or abdominal pain?
No chest or abdominal pain, just the cough and difficulty breathing.
I saw you mentioned COPD. Are there any other health conditions I should know about?
Yes, I also have pulmonary hypertension and gout.
Any medication or food allergies?
No, I don't have any known allergies.
Are you taking any prescribed medications currently?
I take atorvastatin for cholesterol.
Have you had any recent falls, trauma, or substance use in the last 24 hours?
No, nothing like that recently. It started on its own.
Your oxygen saturation is recording at 79% via pulse oximeter.
The current heart rhythm is sinus tachycardia.
With your permission, I'll now assess your skin for temperature, color, or rashes.
Yes, go ahead.
Skin is warm, dry, and appears normal in color with no rashes or discoloration noted.
Are you feeling any pain or discomfort in your head or neck region?
No, I'm not experiencing that either.
Your head is normocephalic, and your pupils are 4mm, equal, round, and reactive to light.
Any drainage, pain, or swelling in your ears, nose, or throat?
No, there's no discharge, pain, or discomfort from those areas.
I'll now examine your chest and listen to your lung sounds.
I hear wheezing, especially in the apices, and decreased breath sounds at the bases.
Chest wall appears intact with no tenderness or deformities noted.
Any tenderness or pain in your abdomen?
No, my abdomen is normal and not painful.
Also, I don't feel any masses or abnormalities in the abdomen or pelvic region.
I'll begin placing you on 4-lead ECG monitoring now.
I'll apply high-flow oxygen via nasal cannula to support your breathing.
Oxygen has been given at 6 liters per minute via nasal cannula.
Importantly, your oxygen saturation has improved to 88%.
We'll administer a nebulizer with Duoneb to reduce the wheezing.
Your strength and coordination in both upper and lower extremities appear normal.
Capnography is initiated now, suggesting hypocapnia from your tachypnea.
We're following the respiratory distress/COPD exacerbation protocol as instructed by the standing order.
To prepare for medications, we're establishing an IV access in your arm.
Dexamethasone has been administered via IV to support your respiratory treatment.
Performing a 12-lead EKG now to closely evaluate your heart rhythm.
Administering 2 grams of magnesium sulfate intravenously now.
Your respiratory rate is monitoring at 42 per minute.
Oxygen saturation remains at 87% currently.
Your blood pressure is at 130 over 69 now.
Heart rate has increased to 132, continuing regular.
CPAP is being applied at 5 cmH2O to assist with your breathing.
Your respiratory rate has reduced slightly to 38, with oxygen saturation improving to 89%.
Blood pressure remains consistent at 126 over 74.
Heart rate is now 127 beats per minute.
You appear alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event (AOx4), with a GCS of 15 recorded.
No evidence of jugular venous distension noted. Eye tracking is normal, without nystagmus.
No swelling, edema, or deformity is observed in the head, neck, or extremities.
No abdominal swelling or tenderness detected during the exam.
We're getting you onto the stretcher now. Let me know if you experience any discomfort.
You are now in the Fowler's position with a shoulder and lap belt for secure transport.
You're currently stable, with no pain and normal coordination.
All EMT crew and patient are secured and ready for transport.
All movements are managed safely to ensure your comfort and stability.
We'll transport you to [COMPANY_NAME], ED Room 15, for definitive treatment.
Vital signs remain stable with no worrisome changes.
A pre-arrival report on your condition has been sent to the hospital team.
Care will be transferred over to the ED staff at [COMPANY_NAME], ED Room 15.
Magnesium sulfate administration is currently in the final stages.
The ambulance has begun its journey.
Your respiratory rate remains at 38, as recorded.
The EKG shows sinus tachycardia with no notable new changes.
We've reached the hospital and the stretcher is now being moved out.
Hospital staff are onsite and prepared for your arrival.
Care now formally transferred to the hospital staff. You're in good hands.
